Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Why Do Some Airlines Still Operate 4-Engine Jets?

Airlines mostly operate legacy quadjets because they are affordable or they have no other choice. Emirates is unusual for just loving its A380s.

Legacy quadjets persist primarily due to low acquisition costs and route-specific requirements rather than operational preference, with Emirates being an exception driven by brand positioning.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Delta Air Lines Holds Flight For Nearly An Hour For Late Passenger Related To Executive

Passengers on a Delta flight claim the aircraft was delayed to accommodate a late-arriving traveler allegedly connected to an airline executive.

Operational inconsistency in gate hold policies risks eroding customer trust and raises questions about fair treatment standards across passenger classes.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The F-35C's operational deployment to the Middle East demonstrates the platform's readiness for contested environments despite ongoing development and sustainment challenges.

See The Aviationist →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Inside The Boeing 777X's Folding Wing Mechanism

Here's how Boeing's engineers have ensured the 777X's iconic folding wingtips can operate safely and reliably.

The 777X's folding wingtips reduce airport gate compatibility issues by 11 feet while maintaining full aerodynamic efficiency at cruise altitude through redundant hydraulic locking systems.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Why Did Boeing Build The 747 With Only A Partial 2nd Deck?

The Boeing 747 has a partial upper deck, used as seating and lounge space, but what's the story behind its distinctive hump?

The 747's iconic hump was a structural compromise: Boeing designed it for eventual cargo conversions, allowing the main deck to remain unobstructed while maintaining structural integrity for freighter operations.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 28, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Organizations with open reporting cultures detect 3-5x more safety issues early, reducing accidents before they occur through proactive SMS implementation.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Why US Airport Gridlock Is Here To Stay – Even With Trump's TSA Pay Order

US airport chaos will persist despite Trump’s TSA pay order, as political gridlock and staffing shortages continue to disrupt travel nationwide.

TSA pay increases alone won't resolve airport congestion; structural staffing gaps and political dysfunction require comprehensive workforce planning beyond salary adjustments.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

The Striking Differences Between Cargo & Passenger Boeing 747s

The Queen of the Skies will forever be an iconic aircraft.

Cargo 747s require structural reinforcements, simplified interiors, and larger door openings that fundamentally alter aerodynamics and handling compared to passenger variants.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

5 Things Passengers Don’t Know About Premium Economy

The cabin continues to become increasingly popular.

Premium economy's growing adoption signals airlines are finding strong unit economics between economy and business class, creating a new revenue tier that improves profitability per seat.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

End Of An Era: Major Airline Abruptly Axes London Heathrow Long-Haul Route

The corridor had only been served (in its most recent iteration) since November.

Rapid route cancellations signal airlines are aggressively pruning unprofitable long-haul services amid persistent capacity pressures and changing demand patterns post-pandemic.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Mar 28, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically identifying and mitigating risks before they materialize into incidents.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Military Mar 28, 2026

5 Critical Performance Metrics That Distinguish The F-22 Raptor From Modern Stealth Rivals

Breaking down how the Raptor reigns supreme.

The F-22's sustained supersonic cruise and sensor fusion capabilities remain unmatched among current stealth fighters, creating operational advantages competitors are still working to replicate.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Airbus Files Patent Reimagining The Business Class Seat: What To Expect

The planemaker has big plans for the reimagining of business class travel.

Airbus's patent filing suggests modular seat designs that could enable airlines to reconfigure cabins more flexibly between high-demand business and premium economy configurations.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's pivot to dedicated UAS operations signals traditional rotorcraft manufacturers are establishing standalone divisions to compete in the rapidly commercializing drone market.

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

Commercial drone manufacturer Quantum Systems is pivoting toward defence contracts, signaling how civilian UAS companies are becoming critical to modern warfare logistics and air defence systems.

Safety Mar 28, 2026

RNO Mustang 9 Departure

Pilots departing from runways 17 left and right at Reno-Tahoe International need to pay special attention to the Mustang 9 Departure. It is extremely important that the procedure on the Mustang 9...

The Mustang 9 departure procedure requires heightened pilot attention due to specific operational constraints that differ from standard Reno-Tahoe departures from runways 17L/R.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

International Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical disruptions in oil markets are forcing airlines to recalibrate fuel surcharge timelines, highlighting how external conflicts directly impact aviation pricing strategies.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

NASA's accelerated Artemis 2 timeline demonstrates confidence in SLS readiness after extensive ground testing and represents a critical validation of deep-space human mission infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers and E-3 AWACS aircraft reduces U.S. aerial refueling and early warning capabilities in the Middle East, potentially constraining sustained military operations in the region.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Deep space communication infrastructure developed over decades of career experience has become mission-critical for NASA's lunar return objectives.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures reflect operational complexity in constrained Bay Area airspace where terrain and proximity to SFO Class B create competing demands for safety and community relations.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines demonstrates commercial lunar logistics becoming operational infrastructure for sustained Artemis exploration rather than occasional missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards program incentivizes aerospace contractors and centers to integrate sustainable practices into operations, potentially establishing new industry standards for environmental stewardship across the aviation sector.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Counterfeit engine parts from theft networks pose undetectable safety risks, requiring enhanced supplier verification protocols beyond standard airworthiness documentation.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

Commercial space industry consolidation continues as specialized suppliers in lunar mobility and power systems gain institutional representation through major trade group membership.

See SpaceNews →

Industry Mar 28, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation sectors can simultaneously address capacity constraints and environmental impacts, requiring integrated technology adoption rather than siloed improvements.

See MDPI →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

Europe's delayed Gateway funding decision amid NASA's redesigned Artemis timeline creates uncertainty for international lunar exploration partnerships and industrial supply chain commitments.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Days showcases the latest operational capabilities and training aircraft of the U.S. Air Force's largest F-35 training hub, providing insight into current military aviation readiness.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables all-weather Earth observation through cloud cover, expanding aviation weather monitoring and terrain mapping capabilities for flight operations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables volcanic monitoring and Earth observation through cloud cover, expanding NASA's disaster preparedness and geological survey capabilities.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Oil pressure loss leading to complete engine failure highlights the critical need for enhanced preflight fluid system inspections and real-time monitoring across general aviation fleets.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's use of commercial rideshare missions reduces launch costs while accelerating technology validation cycles for future space exploration initiatives.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 27, 2026

House Transportation Chair Sam Graves To Retire

Graves helped shape FAA reauthorization, BasicMed and several other general aviation measures.

Loss of a key advocate for general aviation regulatory reform could slow momentum on pending FAA modernization initiatives and medical certification alternatives.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Names Scientists to Support Lunar South Pole Science

NASA has selected 10 participating scientists to help shape a science plan for astronauts to complete on the lunar surface under the Artemis program – including deploying scientific instruments, making critical observations of the landing site, and collecting Moon rocks. “Congratulations to the...

NASA's direct scientist involvement in Artemis lunar missions signals a shift toward research-driven exploration that will inform future crewed spaceflight operations and resource utilization strategies.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

Three killed in Hawaii tour helicopter crash on Kauai

Three people died and two others were injured after an Airborne Aviation tour helicopter crashed on the afternoon…...

Tour helicopter accidents continue despite regulatory oversight, signaling persistent safety challenges in the commercial sightseeing segment that operators must address through enhanced maintenance and pilot training protocols.

I Am Artemis: Michael Guzman

Listen to this audio excerpt from Michael Guzman, Artemis II main propulsion systems engineer: A clue to what Mike Guzman, main propulsion systems engineer at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, loves most can be found in the signature of his work email: a complex string of equations for rocket...

Critical propulsion system expertise at Kennedy Space Center demonstrates NASA's deep technical bench for sustained lunar exploration missions.
